summary: Matched fighter function ft_80081A00 in src/melee/ft/ft_0819.c, replacing a placeholder with C that scans item GObjs, tests item hurtbox overlap against fighter damage ECB data, records a touching item/source player relationship, calls ftCo_800C703C when a hit/contact flag is set, and returns a bool result. The PR also tightened related declarations and field types: ft_80081A00 now takes Fighter_GObj* and returns bool, Fighter.dmg.x1950 became bool, Item.xCFC became HSD_GObj*, and lb_80014638 gained a typed bool prototype.
what_changed: Implemented ft_80081A00 as a bool function taking Fighter_GObj* instead of leaving it as a placeholder stub.
Added a static inline helper named inlineA0 that iterates HSD_GObj_Entities->items using HSD_GObjGetNext, filters items with it_8026BC68, and checks overlap with lb_80014638.
The matched logic computes fp->dmg.x193c from coll_data.cur_topn, coll_data.xA4_ecbCurrCorrect left/right x values, cur_pos.y, and co_attrs.x15C.

When an item qualifies, the code sets ip->xCD0 based on item x position relative to fighter x position, stores the fighter gobj into ip->xCFC, copies fp->player_id into ip->xCB0_source_ply, and sets fp->dmg.x1950.
After scanning items, ft_80081A00 calls ftCo_800C703C if fp->dmg.x1950 is true, clears the flag, and returns true; otherwise it returns false.
Updated ft_0819.h to include ft/forward.h and declare ft_80081A00 as bool ft_80081A00(Fighter_GObj* gobj).
Changed Fighter.dmg.x1950 from int to bool to reflect flag usage in the matched function.
Changed Item.xCFC from u32 to HSD_GObj* because the function stores a fighter gobj pointer there.
Typed lb_80014638 as bool lb_80014638(Vec3*, UNK_T), matching its use as a boolean geometric/overlap test against a Vec3-like damage region input.
Updated the assembly listing for ft_0819.s with real addresses, labels, branch targets, and float literals -1 and 1 in sdata2.
smart_moves: Used a local static inline helper to isolate the item-iteration/contact side effect block, which likely helped preserve register allocation and branch structure for matching.
Kept stack-shaping artifacts with PAD_STACK declarations around ft_80081A00 locals, indicating deliberate stack layout control for matching.
Introduced typed aliases and forward declarations rather than leaving raw void/u32 signatures, improving future readability while matching the existing binary behavior.
Modeled x1950 as a bool because the generated code only uses it as a set/test/clear contact flag.
Modeled Item.xCFC as HSD_GObj* because the matched store writes the fighter gobj directly, reducing future casts and clarifying item-to-fighter relationship data.
decomp_lessons: For functions that iterate global GObj lists, matching may require using the exact project access pattern: HSD_GObj_Entities->items plus HSD_GObjGetNext rather than an abstract iterator.
Small inline helpers can be useful for matching complex loops when they preserve the desired callsite/register behavior and keep the top-level function close to assembly structure.
Boolean fields should be promoted from int only when the diff proves set/test/clear semantics; here fp->dmg.x1950 is written true, tested, cleared false, and returned through a bool path.
Pointer-typed struct fields can be recovered from stores in matched code: Item.xCFC receives the Fighter_GObj* argument, supporting HSD_GObj* as the field type.
Stack padding macros are an accepted tactic when local variables alone do not reproduce the original frame layout.
When a library function is used as a branch condition, updating its prototype to bool can make matched C express the assembly intent more directly.
naming_conventions: Unresolved helper name used in this PR: inlineA0, a local static inline named after the ft_80081A00 address suffix rather than domain behavior.
Fighter object pointers use Fighter_GObj* and local names such as fp_gobj or gobj; fighter data pointers use Fighter* fp.
Item object pointers use Item_GObj* cur and item data pointers use Item* ip.
Recovered-but-still-unnamed struct fields retain offset-style names such as x193c, x1950, xCD0, xCFC, and xCB0_source_ply.
Boolean bitfield-style fighter flags are named with offset/bit suffixes such as x2219_b1, x2223_b4, and x2223_b5.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used PAD_STACK(8 * 4) and PAD_STACK(14 * 4) in ft_80081A00 to influence stack frame layout around local Vec3 sp64 and temporary floats.
Copied fp->coll_data.cur_topn into a local Vec3 before using its x component, matching the assembly's stack copy of values from offsets 0x6F4, 0x6F8, and 0x6FC.
Preserved the branch shape of early exits by gating the body with !fp->x2219_b1 && !fp->x2223_b4 and returning false at the end.
Used a ternary expression ip->pos.x > fp->cur_pos.x ? -1.0f : +1.0f to match the two sdata2 constants ft_804D83A0 and ft_804D83A4.

Assembly output changed from placeholder relative branch/call addresses to resolved labels and calls such as it_8026BC68, lb_80014638, and ftCo_800C703C, indicating the C implementation matched and reduced handwritten assembly burden.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rename inlineA0 or explain the gameplay meaning of ft_80081A00.
Search for other writes to Item.xCFC to confirm whether HSD_GObj* remains the best type.
Search for lb_80014638 callsites to refine its second parameter type beyond UNK_T.
Search for Fighter.dmg.x1930/x193c/x1950 usage to identify the damage/contact subsystem semantics.
